Both past kindnesses and new kindnesses should be remembered by us as reasons to obey. A hearing ear, a seeing eye, and an understanding heart are gifts from God. Everyone who has them receives them from him. God gives not only food and clothing but also wealth and large possessions to many people to whom he does not give grace. Many enjoy these gifts without having hearts that recognize the Giver or the true purpose and use of the gifts. We are bound, in gratitude and interest as well as in duty and faithfulness, to keep the words of the covenant.

The national covenant made with Israel not only symbolized the covenant of grace made with true believers but also represented the outward spreading of the gospel. Those who have been enabled to agree to the Lord’s new covenant of mercy and grace in Jesus Christ and to dedicate themselves to be his people should embrace every opportunity to renew their open declaration of relationship to him and their obligation to him as the God of salvation, living according to it. The sinner is described as someone whose heart turns away from their God; that is where the trouble begins, in the evil heart of unbelief, which leads people to turn from the living God to lifeless idols. Even now people are tempted into this sin when drawn aside by their own desires and imaginations. Such people are roots that produce bitterness and poison. They are weeds that, if left unchecked, will spread over the entire field. Satan may temporarily disguise this bitter taste so you do not experience its natural flavor, but at the last day, or before then, the true taste will be recognized. Notice the sinner’s security in sin. Even when they hear the words of the curse, they still think themselves safe from God's wrath. There is scarcely a threat in all of God’s book more terrible than this. Oh that presumptuous sinners would read it and tremble! for it is a true declaration of God’s wrath against ungodliness and wickedness.

Idolatry would be the ruin of their nation. It is not new for God to bring devastating judgments on people who are close to him in profession. He never does this without good reason. We should seek out the reason so that we may give glory to God and take warning ourselves. Thus, the law of Moses leaves sinners under a curse and uprooted from the Lord’s land; but the grace of Christ toward repentant, believing sinners plants them again in their land; and they will never be uprooted again, being kept by God's power.

Moses ends his prophecy of the Jews’ rejection just as Paul ends his discussion on the same subject when it began to be fulfilled. We are forbidden from curiously investigating the secret plans of God and determining them ourselves. But we are directed and encouraged to diligently seek what God has made known. He has withheld nothing that is beneficial for us, but only what it is good for us to remain ignorant of. The purpose of all divine revelation is not to provide curious subjects for speculation and discussion, but so that we may do all the words of this law and be blessed in our actions. This is plainly revealed in the Bible; beyond this, a person cannot profitably go. By this light one may live and die comfortably and be happy forever.
